questions.text: July 2026: a rooftop comfort-cooling unit holding 40 lbs of R-454B is losing charge. Under the EPA's refrigerant-management (ERR) requirements now in force, what applies?

options.t: It is covered: appliances with ≥15 lbs of HFC/substitute refrigerant must be repaired if the annualized leak rate exceeds 10% for comfort cooling
Nothing — leak-repair requirements only apply to ozone-depleting refrigerants like R-22
options.why: That was true before the AIM Act era. As of Jan 1 2026, leak-repair obligations extend to HFCs and substitutes (GWP > 53) at a 15-lb charge threshold — R-454B qualifies.

options.t: It is covered, but the comfort-cooling trigger rate is 30% per year
options.why: 30% is the INDUSTRIAL PROCESS trigger. Comfort cooling is the strictest: 10%.

options.t: It is exempt because the charge is under the 50-lb threshold that applies to all appliances
options.why: 50 lbs was the old ODS-era threshold. The 2024 ERR rule set 15 lbs for HFC-era appliances — a major 2026 exam update.

questions.why: The 2024 ERR rule (leak-repair provisions effective Jan 1 2026) covers appliances with ≥15 lbs of HFC or substitute refrigerant with GWP > 53. Triggers: 10% comfort cooling · 20% commercial refrigeration · 30% industrial process.
questions.ref: EPA Emissions Reduction & Reclamation rule (2024), leak-repair subpart

questions.text: July 2026: a supply house still has brand-new R-410A split-system components on the shelf (manufactured before January 1, 2025). For a new residential install, a contractor may:

options.t: Install them — EPA's May 2026 reconsideration rule removed the January 1, 2026 installation deadline, so pre-2025-built systems may be installed until supplies run out
Not install them — installing new R-410A systems became illegal nationwide on January 1, 2026
options.why: That WAS the original rule — which is exactly why this distractor works. EPA bridged the deadline with enforcement discretion in 2025 and then permanently removed it in the May 2026 reconsideration rule.

options.t: Install them, and OEMs may also resume building new R-410A condensing units while demand lasts
options.why: Half the update swallowed the other half: the manufacture/import prohibition (GWP ≥ 700, from Jan 1 2025) still stands — only the downstream INSTALLATION deadline was removed.

options.t: Not install them, and existing R-410A systems can no longer be recharged either
options.why: Doubly overcorrected: installs of pre-2025-built stock are allowed again, and servicing/recharging the existing installed base was never prohibited.

questions.why: A genuinely 2026 fact: the manufacture/import ban (GWP ≥ 700) from Jan 1 2025 still stands, but EPA's reconsideration final rule (published May 26, 2026) removed the Jan 1 2026 installation cutoff — R-410A systems built from pre-2025 components may be installed until stock runs out. Stay current: the rules moved twice in 18 months.
questions.ref: EPA Technology Transitions reconsideration final rule (May 2026); 40 CFR 84 Subpart B

questions.text: What federal certification do you need to purchase and handle R-32 and R-454B in 2026?

options.t: EPA Section 608 certification — the venting/handling rules extend to substitutes; A2L safety training is expected by employers and suppliers but is not a separate federal card
A new "EPA A2L endorsement" that must be added to your 608 card
options.why: No such federal endorsement exists. A2L training programs (ESCO, OEM, ACCA) are industry requirements, not an EPA certification category.

options.t: None — A2Ls are not ozone-depleting, so Section 608 does not apply to them
options.why: Section 608's prohibitions and sales restrictions reach substitutes, and the 2026 refrigerant-management regime explicitly covers HFC/A2L refrigerants. 608 certification is required.

options.t: Only Universal certification holders may handle A2Ls; Type II is insufficient for split systems
options.why: The Type I/II/III/Universal structure is about equipment category (small appliances / high-pressure / low-pressure), not refrigerant flammability class. Type II covers residential split systems regardless of A2L.

questions.why: Section 608 remains the federal certification for regulated refrigerants including A2L substitutes. Expect employers, distributors and AHJs to also require documented A2L safety training — but that is industry practice layered on top, not a new EPA license.
questions.ref: EPA Section 608 program; ESCO A2L training program
